coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots with tunable emission for multifaceted application: solvatochromism, moisture sensing, pH sensing, and solid state multicolor lighting
coredata.dc:creator: Moniruzzaman, Md
Kim, Jongsung
coredata.dc:description: Novel N-doped green-emitting carbon dots (N-GCDs) that were responsive to various external stimuli and had high quantum yields (QYs) for multifaceted applications were synthesized from β-resorcylic acid and ethylenediamine using a hydrothermal process followed by a reflux method. The QYs of the N-GCDs were in the range of 17.36%–48.4% and were associated with different photoluminescent color emission in different solvents. Interestingly, the as-synthesized N-GCDs exhibited strict solvent polarity-dependent switchability (solvatochromism) and multicolor excitation-wavelength-independent emission in different solvents. Sensing of an analyte based on the switching of the photoluminescence (PL) emission maxima (λmax) is always preferred over that based on fluorescence quenching to avoid complexity and confusion regarding the initial fluorescence intensity (I0) and thus make the sensing process simple, rapid, ordered, and reliable. The N-GCDs exhibited blue emission in low-polarity organic solvents, but with the increase of the trace water content in the system, the PL emission peak gradually shifted to a higher wavelength (bathochromic shift) at a single-wavelength excitation. Thus, the N-GCDs are sensitive visual probes for the fluorescence detection of the trace water content in organic solvents without sophisticated processes. They also indicate the pH-response behavior. When the pH decreased from 7.6 to 2.4, the fluorescence emission intensity decreased, with a particularly good linear relationship (R2 = 0.990) in the range of 3.8–6.2. Moreover, the polarity-dependent-emission property was observed in the solid state polymer matrices. Multicolor-emissive N-GCD/polymer films were prepared by integrating N-GCDs with the polymer matrices of different polarity, demonstrating the application of carbon dots for solid-state lighting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
High quantum yield
Solvatochromism
H2O detection in organic solvents
pH sensing
Multicolor N-GCD/polymer film

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of water content in acetone/THF using N-GCDs probe
Detection of pH using N-GCDs probe
Detection of moisture content in organic solvents
N-GCD/polymer films for multicolor solid-state lighting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (inset) Gaussian particle-size distribution of as-prepared N-GCDs, (b) HR-TEM image, (c) XRD pattern of as-prepared N-GCDs, and (d) Raman spectra of N-GCDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of β-resorcylic acid and N-GCDs; high-resolution (b) C1 s, (c) N1 s, and (d) O1 s XPS spectra of the N-GCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of β-resorcylic acid (i) and N-GCDs (ii); PL excitation (iii) and PL emission (iv) spectra of N-GCDs. (b) PL emission spectra of N-GCDs in DI water at different excitation wavelengths (300–460 nm).

(a) Normalized PL emission spectra at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, (b) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (c) PL emission maxima (λmax) with respect to the relative solvent polarity parameter (ET N) of N-GCDs in 16 solvents of different polarity.

PL emission spectra, normalized PL emission spectra, and a plot of the PL emission maxima (λmax) vs. the water content for N-GCDs dispersed in acetone ((a), (b), and (c), respectively) and in THF ((d), (e), and (f), respectively). The excitation wavelength was 390 nm.

(a) PL emission spectra of N-GCDs dispersed in the citric acid-sodium phosphate buffer with respect to the pH (7.6 to 2.4). (b) Plot of the PL emission intensity at 520 nm vs. the pH in the citric acid-sodium phosphate buffer. The excitation wavelength was 390 nm.
Normalized PL emission spectra of three N-GCD/polymer films. The insets show corresponding photographs taken at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

Schematic elucidation for the synthesis of N-GCDs and their multifaceted application: (a) solvatochromism, (b) detection of the water content in organic solvents, (c) multicolor N-GCD/polymer films for solid-state lighting, and (d) pH detection.
Formation mechanism of the as-synthesized bright green-emitting N-GCDs.
Comparison of the N-GCDs with other fluorescent probes used for solvatochromism.

row.entry: Moisture content detection, pH sensing and N-CD/polymer films

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots with tunable emission for multifaceted application: solvatochromism, moisture sensing, pH sensing, and solid state multicolor lighting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam-daero, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel N-doped green-emitting carbon dots (N-GCDs) that were responsive to various external stimuli and had high quantum yields (QYs) for multifaceted applications were synthesized from β-resorcylic acid and ethylenediamine using a hydrothermal process followed by a reflux method. The QYs of the N-GCDs were in the range of 17.36%–48.4% and were associated with different photoluminescent color emission in different solvents. Interestingly, the as-synthesized N-GCDs exhibited strict solvent polarity-dependent switchability (solvatochromism) and multicolor excitation-wavelength-independent emission in different solvents. Sensing of an analyte based on the switching of the photoluminescence (PL) emission maxima (λmax) is always preferred over that based on fluorescence quenching to avoid complexity and confusion regarding the initial fluorescence intensity (I0) and thus make the sensing process simple, rapid, ordered, and reliable. The N-GCDs exhibited blue emission in low-polarity organic solvents, but with the increase of the trace water content in the system, the PL emission peak gradually shifted to a higher wavelength (bathochromic shift) at a single-wavelength excitation. Thus, the N-GCDs are sensitive visual probes for the fluorescence detection of the trace water content in organic solvents without sophisticated processes. They also indicate the pH-response behavior. When the pH decreased from 7.6 to 2.4, the fluorescence emission intensity decreased, with a particularly good linear relationship (R2 = 0.990) in the range of 3.8–6.2. Moreover, the polarity-dependent-emission property was observed in the solid state polymer matrices. Multicolor-emissive N-GCD/polymer films were prepared by integrating N-GCDs with the polymer matrices of different polarity, demonstrating the application of carbon dots for solid-state lighting.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are excellent multifunctional materials that have been extensively explored in the past few years because of their low toxicity, high photostability, excellent dispersibility in various solvents, biocompatibility, ease of preparation from less toxic chemicals or ecofriendly natural resources, and good photoluminescence (PL) property [1–6]. Owing to these superior properties of CDs compared with semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CDs have found extensive use in sensing [7,8], bioimaging [9,10], photocatalysis [11], medical imaging [12], light-emitting diodes [13], photovoltaics [14], antibacterial agents [15], and drug delivery [16]. Thus far, CDs have been synthesized from natural biomass, the carbonization of small non-aromatic open/closed chain molecules, weak organic acids, carbohydrates, amino acids, urea, ethylene diamine, etc. However, they often suffer from low quantum yields (QYs) and show an excitation-dependent PL emission property, restricting their application in many fields. Nowadays, substituted polycyclic hydrocarbon/substituted benzene is employed as a CD source to increase the QY, e.g., folic acid [17], diaminonaphthalene [18], 1,3-dihydroxynaphthalene [19], phenylenediamine [20], and 1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene [21]. CDs derived from these compounds often show emission maxima that redshift with excitation-independent emission. After many years of effort, CDs with high QYs for blue (94.5%) [17], green (73%) [18], yellow (76%) [22], and red (53%) [19] emissions have been successfully synthesized using substituted benzene/substituted polycyclic hydrocarbon. It is essential to synthesize multicolor-emissive CDs with a tunable PL using a simple method. There are several reports of the successful preparation of multicolor-emissive CDs with tunable PL by controlling the particle sizes and surface states, but these processes require complex reaction conditions and adjustment of the reactants [23–25]. Thus, a more facile route is highly desirable.

The commonly observed excitation-dependent emission in CDs is often followed by an attenuation in the emission intensity [26]. Nevertheless, Pan et al. synthesized truly excitation-dependent emissive fluorescent CDs using formamide and citric acid via microwave irradiation [27]. Currently, the research focus is the development of CDs with excitation-independent PL emission that can be tuned by various external stimuli for multifaceted application, especially in the field of sensing. It is very easy and efficient to regulate the PL properties of CDs via the polarity-dependent solvent effect. Thus far, only a few deliberations have been reported regarding the solvent polarity effects on CDs. For example, Wang et al. prepared excitation-independent but solvent-dependent CDs (green–red; QY: 10%–35%) from p-phenylenediamine [6]. Chao et al. prepared excitation-independent but solvent-dependent CDs (green–blue; QY: 7.7%–38.3%) from o-phenylenediamine [3]. Pan et al. synthesized highly luminescent blue-emitting carbon nanoparticles (QY: 31.6%–40.6%) from ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid salts via a pyrolytic route [28]. Yang et al. prepared solvent-sensitive strongly green-emitting graphene quantum dots (QY: 11.5%) from graphene oxide [29].

Water is a common impurity in organic solvents, and its quantitative detection via the fluorescent probe-based analytical method is very important in several chemical and industrial fields [30,31]. Water contamination in organic solvents may cause hydrolysis and form unwanted oxidation products, damaging the organic solvents during storage [32,33]. Karl–Fisher titration is the conventional method for measuring the water content in organic solvents but has drawbacks; e.g., it requires toxic reagents, precise sample manipulation, and specified equipment [34]. Fluorescent organic dyes, conjugated fluorescent polymers, and fluorescent metalorganic frameworks are ideal substitutes for classical Karl–Fisher titration for the detection of trace water. For example, Yang et al. performed a fluorescent organic dye-based assay for the detection of the water content [35]. Chi et al. synthesized dual-emissive CDs/Eu-MOF nanohybrids for the detection of water in organic solvents [36]. However, these methods often suffer from disadvantages, such as toxicity, complex synthesis procedures, long synthesis periods, and photostability. Thus, interest has grown towards the synthesis of new CD-based fluorescent sensors with tunable emission spectra for detection of water [3,37].

Many biological and chemical reactions are controlled at an optimum pH condition. Recently, attempts have been made to use pH-dependent CDs with excitation-independent emission for intercellular pH monitoring and cellular imaging. For example, Dong et al. reported orange-emitting N-doped CDs (N-CDs) with excitation-independent emission for pH sensing and intercellular pH imaging [38]. However, the N-CDs exhibited a sigmoidal curve with two fluctuations corresponding to two pKa values, which limits their applications with regard to their linearity and precise pH monitoring. In another study, the same group synthesized N-CDs with excitation-independent emission, showing a linear response over the pH range of 4.45–7.00, but their low fluorescence intensity near the physiological pH limits their application [39]. It is necessary to develop a pH sensor having a single pKa value with high intensity near the physiological pH for intercellular pH monitoring.

In the present study, novel N-GCDs with high QYs and excitation-independent emission for multifaceted applications were synthesized using β-resorcylic acid and ethylenediamine using a simple hydrothermal process followed by a reflux method (Scheme 1 ). Polarity-dependent PL emission was observed in different solvents and polymer matrices at a single excitation wavelength. Blue emission was observed in low-polarity organic solvents, but with the increase of the trace water content, the emission peak gradually shifted to a higher wavelength (bathochromic shift). Thus, the N-GCDs are sensitive visual probes for fluorescence detection of the trace water content in organic solvents without sophisticated processes. Moreover, the polarity-dependent emission property was observed in solid-state polymer matrices. Multicolor-emissive N-GCD/polymer films were prepared by integrating the N-GCDs with polymer matrices of different polarity, broadening the application of CDs for solid-state lighting. Additionally, the N-GCDs exhibited sensitive pH-response behavior. When the pH decreased from 7.6 to 2.4, the fluorescence emission intensity decreased, with a particularly good linear relationship (R2 = 0.99) in the range of 3.8–6.2.

2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid, i.e., β-resorcylic acid (C7H6O4); ethylenediamine (C2H8N2); toluene; tetrahydrofuran (THF); dichloromethane (DCM); acetone; N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF); acetonitrile (CH3CN); dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO); 2-propanol; 1-butanol; 1-propanol; ethanol; methanol; ethylene glycol; glycerol; polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, molecular weight (MW) = 89,000–98,000); polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, MW = 10,000); polystyrene (PS, MW = 35,000); quinine sulfate; sulfuric acid (H2SO4); sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4); and citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O) were bought from Sigma–Aldrich, USA. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the synthesis process and experiments. All chemicals used in the experiment were of analytical quality and employed without farther purification.

PL spectra and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature using a PL spectrometer (Quanta Master, Photon Technology International, NJ, USA) and UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 100), respectively. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of the as-prepared N-GCDs were obtained using a microscope (JEM 3010, JEOL Ltd., Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a high-resolution FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Vertex 70). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an X-ray source with a twin-anode (Al-Kα, hν = 1486.6 eV) gun and a monochromatic gun. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected using a Smart Lab instrument (Rigaku) with a 4-kW X-ray generator and a D/teX Ultra 250 detector. Raman spectra were recorded via micro-Raman spectroscopy (ANDOR Monora500i, 633 nm). The pH values of the CD dispersion in buffers were recorded using a pH meter (Thermo Orion 3 star). 1H-NMR and 13C–NMR spectra were recorded using Bruker Advance III 500 MHz instrument. Tetramethylsilane (TMS) was used as an internal reference and methanol-d4 was used as a solvent to record the corresponding NMR spectra. The chemical shifts are represented in δ (ppm units) relative to TMS.

In a typical synthesis procedure, 1 mL of ethylenediamine and 500 mg of β-resorcylic acid were dissolved completely in 30 mL of DI water in a beaker. The resultant solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 190 ℃ for 15 h in a hot air oven. The solution was transferred to 50-mL glass vials after cooling naturally to room temperature. The resultant yellowish-brown solution was refluxed at 80 ℃ for 48 h under constant stirring, and the color changed to deep reddish-brown, indicating the formation of green-emitting N-GCDs. After cooling to room temperature, centrifugation of the solution was performed at 10,000 rpm for 20 min, to discard the agglomerated particles. Then, the N-GCDs dispersion was filtered with a 0.22 μM filter (three filtration cycles) and the solvent was evaporated using high pressure rotary evaporator at 80 ℃. Finally, N-GCDs were dispersed in ethanol and dried at 70 ℃ to obtain N-GCD powder, which was dispersed in different solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in acetone/THF using N-GCDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experiment, a series of acetone solution of different water composition were prepared keeping the concentration (0.1 mg/mL) of N-GCDs constant. All the PL readings were recorded after 5 min of solution preparation at room temperature. Similar experimental conditions were employed for detection of water content in THF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of pH using N-GCDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run, solutions with different pH values in the range of 2.4–7.6 were prepared in a citric acid-sodium phosphate buffer (pH buffer capacity of 2.6–7.6) using sodium phosphate dibasic (0.2 M) and citric acid monohydrate (0.1 M). The PL emission spectra of the N-GCD dispersion (0.1 mg/mL) in buffers of different pH were recorded at room temperature.

The N-GCDs were prepared using β-resorcylic acid and ethylenediamine as precursor through a facile hydrothermal method followed by a reflux method. The reaction of carboxylic acid and ethylenediamine under high temperature condensation process yields imidazolidine/ imidazoline moiety via nucleophilic addition [40,41]. Then the compound further undergo Schiff base condensation and carbonization. 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectra of the precursor β-resorcylic acid and N-GCDs were recorded and different proton/carbon environments were compared to predict the formation mechanism. Fig. S1 (electronic supplementary information (ESI†)) shows the 1H-NMR spectra of N-GCDs that contain several new peaks in the region of 2.8–3.5 ppm due the sp3 carbon environment (including imidazolidine moiety carbon protons in the range of 3.2–3.5 ppm) which were absent in the 1H-NMR spectra (Fig. S1 (ESI†)) of precursor β-resorcylic acid. Sharp peak around 5 ppm is due to amino proton. Several peaks around 6.1–8.2 ppm are due to sp2 carbon core protons and hydroxyl protons. Fig. S2 (ESI†) shows the 13 C-NMR spectra of N-GCDs that also contain several new peaks with respect to 13C-NMR spectra (Fig. S2 (ESI†) of the precursor β-resorcylic acid confirming that N-GCDs contain number of new carbon environments. Peaks around 40–50 ppm are due to sp3 carbon atoms (including imidazolidine moiety carbons environment). Peaks around 100–170 ppm are due to sp2 carbons core and carboxylic acid functional groups. Both 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectral data are in support of the presence of imidazolidine moiety in the N-GCDs that indicate the proposed plausible synthesis pathway mechanism as shown in Scheme 2 .

The structure and morphology of the as-synthesized N-GCDs were analyzed using HR-TEM and XRD. The TEM images (Fig. 1 a and Fig. S3 ESI†) show that the particles were well dispersed, with an average size of approximately 12–22 nm, which is in accordance with previous reports [42,43]. Fig. 1b shows an HR-TEM image of the N-GCDs, indicating weekly discernible lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, which suggests that the particles are weekly crystalline. This is attributed to the 001 facet of graphene, in accordance with the XRD peak (Fig. 1c) around 23° [44,45]. To investigate the intrinsic structure of the N-GCDs, Raman spectra (Fig. 1d) were obtained, which showed the common features within the range of 1,000–2,000 cm−1. The peak at 1,572 cm−1 corresponds to the G-band, indicating the sp2 carbon networks. The peak centered at 1,340 cm−1 corresponds to the D-band, indicating the existence of defects in the carbon networks or defects resulting from the oxidized surface [24,46].

To investigate the surface functional groups of the N-GCDs, FT-IR analysis was performed. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the FT-IR spectra contain a broad peak around 3,170–3400 cm−1. The peaks around 3,175 and 3,350 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching frequencies of N-H and O-H bonds, respectively [3]. There are also stretching and bending vibrations of methylene (−CH2) around 2,960, 2,925, and 2,855 cm−1 [39]. The stretching-vibration peaks at 1,666 and 1,597 cm−1 are assigned to CO and CC/CN, respectively. The peaks around 1,224 and 1,144 cm−1 may be assigned to CC and CO, respectively. The peaks around 1,464 and 1,300 cm−1 are ascribed to C–N bond stretching [6]. The several new peaks compared with the precursor β-resorcylic acid indicate that the N-GCDs were successfully formed. These results reveal the presence of various oxygen- and nitrogen-containing surface functional groups or chemical bonds, such as −OH, −NH2, −COOH, methylene, aromatic CC, CN, CO, CC, CN, and CO. XPS spectra were used to further analyze the surface information of the N-GCDs, and the results were consistent with the FT-IR data. Fig. S4 (ESI†) shows the total survey spectrum of the N-GCDs, exhibiting three typical peaks: C1s (285 eV), N 1s (399.4 eV), and O 1s (532.3 eV). In the C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b), the three distinct peaks at 284.6, 286.1, and 287.8 eV are assigned to CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO/CN, respectively [38,47]. In the case of N1 s (Fig. 2c), there are two peaks at 399.2 and 400.8 eV, which are attributed to amino-N and pyridini C-N [48], respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the O1 s band (Fig. 2d) contains three peaks at 531, 532.2, 533.3 eV, which are attributed to CO, CO, and COH/COC, respectively [49].

The optical properties of the as-synthesized N-GCDs were investigated using UV–vis absorbance and PL spectra. The N-GCDs exhibited good dispersibility in a wide range of solvents, such as toluene, THF, ethyl acetate, DCM, acetone, DMF, acetonitrile, DMSO, 2-propanol, 1-butanol, 1-propanol, ethanol, methanol, ethylene glycol, glycerol, and water. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and PL emission spectra of the N-GCDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-synthesized N-GCDs dispersed in water exhibited a characteristic sharp band at 278 nm assigned to π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 carbogenic core (CC), along with a weak band around 328 nm assigned to the n–π* transition of the CN/CO bonds. The broad band centered around 420 nm is attributed to the typical n–π* transition of surface functional groups (carboxyl and/or CN bonds), which leads to strong green emission [50,51]. Fig. 3a also shows the maximum PL excitation wavelength (λex = 390 nm) and emission wavelength (λem = 520 nm) of the N-GCDs dispersed in water.

Additionally, the PL emission spectra of the N-GCDs at different excitation wavelengths were examined, as shown in Fig. 3b. The N-GCDs showed nearly excitation-independent behavior when the excitation wavelength was changed from 300 to 460 nm, probably because of the homogeneous surface state [46].

Interestingly, the N-GCDs exhibited strict solvent polarity-dependent switchability and multicolor excitation-independent PL emission in different solvents. The PL emission spectra and UV–vis absorbance spectra of the N-GCD dispersion in 16 solvents of different polarity—ranging from toluene (lowest polarity) to water (highest polarity)—were recorded. Fig. 4 a shows the normalized PL emission maxima (λmax) of the N-GCDs dispersed in the 16 solvents, clearly indicating a bathochromic shift of the PL emission maxima from 440 nm (blue) to 520 nm (green) with the increase of the solvent polarity at the single wavelength excitation of 390 nm. Table S1 (ESI†) shows the PL emission maxima of the N-GCDs with respect to the relative polarity parameter (ET N) of the solvents. A linear plot of the PL emission maxima vs. the relative solvent polarity was fitted, as shown in Fig. 4b. The plot shows an excellent linear relationship between the bathochromic shift (redshift) and the solvent polarity. Hence, the N-GCDs are an excellent tool for measuring the solvent polarity, with a detection limit of 0.12. Fig. 4c shows the UV–vis absorption spectral records of the N-GCDs dispersed in the solvents of different polarity. Distinct absorption bands are located at 402.3 nm (toluene), 404.1 nm (THF), 404.7 nm (ethyl acetate), 405.9 nm (DCM), 408.5 nm (acetone), 414.0 nm (DMF), 408.6 nm (acetonitrile), 419.0 nm (DMSO), 425.0 nm (2-propanol), 425.0 nm (butanol), 425.0 nm (1-propanol), 425.0 nm (ethanol), 425.0 nm (methanol), 430.0 nm (ethylene glycol), and 420.0 nm (water). The N-GCDs also exhibited an apparent bathochromic shift in the absorption wavelength with the increasing polarity of the solvent, similar to the case of the PL emission maxima, with the exceptions of acetonitrile and water. The irregularity for acetonitrile and water was perhaps due to their relatively poor solubility [7,3]. Interestingly, the optical properties and absorption spectra of the N-GCDs in five different types of alcohols (2-propanol, 1-butanol, 1-propanol, ethanol, and methanol) were almost similar, even though the alcohols had different polarities, as shown in Fig. S5 (ESI†). This is probably due to, hydrogen bonds formation between oxygen and nitrogen atoms on the surface of the N-GCDs [3]. The results clearly indicate that the N-GCDs had different surface states in different solvents, indicating their unique solvent-dependent emission property. It has been proven that the surface states affect the emission mechanism. The solvent-dependent emission properties of CDs were previously investigated by Chadha et al., who proposed that the emission is mainly controlled by the interactions between the protic solvent and CDs via hydrogen bonding, whereas in aprotic solvents, the dipole–dipole interaction plays an important role in the PL emission shift [52–54]. On the other hand, a novel elucidation was proposed by Messina et al. regarding the fluorescence tunability, which revealed that the origin of the fluorescence tunability is dot-to-dot polydispersity, independent of the solvent interactions [55]. We also examined the excitation-dependent emission property of N-GCDs in 16 solvents of different polarity, as shown in Fig. S6 (ESI†). Interestingly, in every solvent, the N-GCDs exhibited strict excitation-independent emission. It is well known that the excitation-independent emission properties of CDs mainly depend on the homogeneous surface state rather than the particle size [46]. This was confirmed in the present study. Furthermore, we followed synthesis strategy maintaining all the reaction conditions unaltered with only precursor β-resorcylic acid (without ethylenediamine) and recorded UV–vis absorption spectra as shown in Fig. S7 (ESI†). Comparing the UV absorption peak, it is obvious that the peak around 420 nm in aqueous dispersion of N-GCDs were due to N-containing surface functional groups or nitrogenous sub-structure (imidazolidine/imidazoline). It is well reported fact that nitrogen containing surface states or molecular substructures on the surface can often be PL center sensitive to pH and solvents [3,6,28,38,39,56–59]. N-GCDs contain nitrogenous surface states or molecular substructures as evident from UV–vis, FTIR, XPS, 1H-NMR and 13C–NMR spectra. So we speculate that the solvent dependent emission property was probably due the change in surface electronic state induced by solvent dipole moment. The surface electronic state is affected more, as the polarity of solvent increases. Thus energy gap is reduced resulting in the bathochromic shift of PL emission wavelength maxima. In the case of alcohols, absorption and emission spectra are less affected with polarity probably due to the formation of strong hydrogen bonds with the nitrogen in those solvents resulting in stabilization of the excited state [6,58,59].

To determine the QYs of the N-GCDs, a comparative method was employed taking quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4, literature QYs: 54%) as a reference sample [60]. The QY measurement experiment is detailed in the Supplementary Information (experimental ESI†). The QYs of the N-GCDs in eight different selected solvents were determined. Fig. S8 (ESI†) shows a plot of the integrated PL intensity vs. the absorbance of the reference sample (quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4) and N-GCDs in different solvents (toluene, THF, acetone, acetonitrile, DMSO, ethanol, ethylene glycol, and water). The detailed results for the QYs of the N-GCDs in the various solvents are presented in Table S2 (ESI†). The highest QY of 48.4% was exhibited by toluene, and the lowest QY of 17.36% was exhibited by water. Obviously, the QYs in all the selected organic solvents were higher than that in water. Thus, the solvent-dependent properties of the N-GCDs can be utilized to achieve high QYs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of moisture content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: A simple, efficient, and rapid tunable fluorescence-based technique was developed to measure the moisture content in low-polarity organic solvents. We studied the changes in the color and spectrum of N-GCDs dispersed in different low-polarity organic solvents by mixing a certain amount of water into the system. When a trace amount of water was added to different organic solvents of low polarity containing N-GCDs, both the emission color and the PL emission maxima (λmax) exhibited a redshift. According to this unique property of the N-GCDs, we investigated their application as visual sensors for detecting the moisture content in organic solvents. Figs. 5 a and 5b show the continuous bathochromic shift of the PL emission maxima with the increase of the moisture content in acetone from 0% to 100%. Interestingly, the bathochromic shift of the emission peak is linear with respect to the moisture content in four different ranges, as shown in Fig. 5c. Zhang et al. reported similar results and attributed the difference in slope to the preferential solvation of O-CDs by acetone (less polar) in the binary mixture of solvents [3]. As shown in Fig. 5c, when the water content was <2%, the sensitivity of the bathochromic shift of the characteristic λmax was extremely high, and a noticeable change in color was noticed upon the adding a trace amount of water. The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.37% by means of signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Another important observation was that with the increase of the water content, the PL intensity gradually decreased. Fig. S9 (ESI†) shows the linear relationship of the PL intensity quenching with the increase of the water content in acetone. We also investigated the detection of the water content in THF. Fig. 5d and 5e show the shift of the PL emission maxima with the increase of the water content in THF from 0% to 100%. A similar plot with four different linear regions is shown in Fig. 5f. The limit of detection in THF was 0.36%. For the first time, we employed N-GCDs for sensing the trace moisture content in organic solvents via solvatochromism. The most interesting unique features of the N-GCDs were applied to determine the purity of the organic solvents by detecting the trace water content. Even a trace moisture content can hamper the activities of many chemicals/drugs and reduce the product yield in many organic reactions; thus, detecting the water content in organic solvents is important. Table 1 shows a comparison of the fluorescent probes used for solvatochromism, indicating the novelty and superiority of our N-
GCD probe.

Many biological and chemical reactions are controlled at an optimum pH condition. The pH plays a crucial role in many biological and physiological processes, such as apoptosis, cellular proliferation, homeostasis, enzymatic activity, and tumor growth; hence, it is very important to monitor the pH. Interestingly, the N-GCDs exhibited an excellent pH-dependent property in the acidic pH range, making them a potential fluorescent nanomaterial for pH detection. The detailed experiments are discussed in Section 2.5. Fig. 6 a shows that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the decrease of the pH from 7.6 to 2.4 under excitation at 390 nm. Fig. 6b shows the sigmoidal curve obtained by plotting the PL emission intensity at 520 nm vs. the pH, indicating that the PL emission intensity decreased significantly with the decrease of the pH. Using the sigmoidal curve, the pKa value was calculated to be approximately 5.2. Fig. S10 (ESI†) shows the effective linear response plot in the pH range of 3.8–6.2 with R2 = 0.99.

To check the PL emission reversibility with respect to pH of the medium, the aqueous solution containing N-GCDs was adjusted between 7.6 and 2.4 using hydrochloric acid and aqueous sodium hydroxide in several cycles and the fluorescence intensity of N-GCDs were measured in each steps. As shown in Fig. S11 (ESI†) the PL emission intensity of N-GCDs probe was reversible with respect to pH of the medium. Fig. S12 (ESI†) shows a photograph of the N-GCD dispersion at pH values of 7.6 and 2.6 under UV light (excitation at 365 nm). Furthermore, UV–vis absorption spectra were tested under different pH condition from 2.4 to 7.6 as shown in Fig. S13 (ESI†). It is observed that the peak around 420 nm were red shifted with increase in the acidity of the solution. This result indicates that the change in pH leads to variation in the surface electronic state of N-GCDs. The change in absorption and PL spectra of N-GCDs at different pH suggest that the surface states are mostly in control for their PL emission [56,57]. Nitrogen containing surface states or molecular substructures on the surface can often be PL center sensitive to pH and solvents [3,6,28,38,39,56–59]. N-GCDs contain nitrogenous surface states or molecular substructures as evident from UV–vis, FTIR, XPS, 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR. It is very much obvious that the lone pair of nitrogen form co-ordination bond with H+ in the acidic environment. So it can be speculated that the fluorescence switching of N-GCDs with pH was probably due to protonation and deprotonation of surface nitrogen. With variation in pH, the protonation and deprotonation behavior also changes which in turn change the surface electronic states responsible for PL emission that is supported by absorption spectra of N-GCDs at different pH condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-GCD/polymer films for multicolor solid-state lighting
ce:section.ce:para: Motivated by the polarity-dependent emission property of the N-GCDs in a liquid state (in solvents), we attempted to extend the application of the N-GCDs for solid-state lighting by incorporating the N-GCDs into polymer matrices. For this purpose, three polymers of different polarity-PS, PVP, and PVA-with functional groups similar to those of the aforementioned solvents were chosen. The preparation of the N-GCD/polymer films is detailed in the Supplementary Information (experimental ESI†). As shown in Fig. 7 , the PL emission properties of the N-GCD/polymer films were investigated. PL emission maxima occurred at 435 nm (N-GCD/PS), 455 nm (N-GCD/PVP), and 505 nm (N-GCD/PVA) under the single wavelength excitation of 390 nm. Fig. S14 (ESI†) shows photographs of the three different N-GCD/polymer (N-GCD/PS, N-GCD/PVP, and N-GCD/PVA) films in UV light and daylight, clearly indicating the polarity-dependent solid-state emission under UV irradiation, i.e., blue-violet (PS), blue (PVP), and green (PVA). Thus, the polarity-dependent optical features of the N-GCDs in the solution state can be extended to solid-state polymer matrices for solid-state lighting.

Novel green-emitting N-GCDs with high QYs and strict excitation-independent emission for multifaceted applications were synthesized using β-resorcylic acid and ethylene diamine via a simple hydrothermal process followed by a reflux method. The N-GCDs exhibited strict polarity-dependent emission at a single wavelength excitation when they were dispersed in solvents of different polarity or polymer matrices. The QY of the N-GCDs reached 48.4% in toluene. Blue emission was observed in low-polarity organic solvents, but with the addition of a trace amount of water to the system, the emission peak gradually shifted to a higher wavelength (bathochromic shift). Thus, the N-GCDs are sensitive visual probes for fluorescence detection of the trace water content in organic solvents without sophisticated processes. Moreover, multicolor-emissive N-GCD/polymer films were achieved by integrating the N-GCDs with polymer matrices of different polarity, broadening their application for solid-state lighting. In addition, the N-GCDs exhibited sensitive pH-response behavior in the acidic pH range. When the pH decreased from 7.6 to 2.4, the fluorescence emission intensity decreased, with a particularly good linear relationship (R2 = 0.99) in the range of 3.8–6.2.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the GRRC program of Gyeonggi Province (GRRC Gachon 2017-B01), the Human Resource Development of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP), and the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) of the Republic of Korea (20174030201530).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.05.035.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Md. Moniruzzaman is a doctoral student under the guidance of Prof. Jongsung Kim and his research interests include the synthesis of carbon based novel fluorescent nanomaterials and their application such as biosensors, polymer film, catalysis and renewable energy.

Jongsung Kim received his Ph.D. in 1997 from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, USA. He is currently serving as a Professor in the department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, South Korea. His research interests include Nanomaterials, biosensors, catalysis, and renewable energy.
